messages.content:
Marilyn McCord Adams and Stewart
I--Marilyn McCord Adams
I
Introduction. Over the past thirty years, analytic philosophers of religion have defined 'the problem of evil' in terms of the prima facie difficulty in consistently maintaining
God exists, and is omnipotent, omniscient, and
perfectly good
and
Evil exists.
In a crisp and classic article 'Evil and Omnipotence',' J. L. Mackie emphasized that the problem is not that (1) and (2) are logically inconsistent by themselves, but that they together with quasi-logical rules formulating attribute-analyses--such as
) A perfectly good being would always eliminate evil so far as it could,
and
) There are no limits to what an omnipotent being can do,
-constitute an inconsistent premiss-set. He added, of course, that the inconsistency might be removed by substituting alternative and perhaps more subtle analyses, but cautioned that such replacements of (P1) and (P2) would save 'ordinary theism' from his charge of positive irrationality, only if true to its 'essential requirements'.2
In an earlier paper 'Problems of Evil: More Advice to Christian Philosophers',3 I underscored Macki it a step further. In debates about whether th evil can establish the irrationality of religious belief, care must be taken, both by the atheologians who de believers who defend against it, to insure that the operative attribute-analyses accurately reflect that religion’s understanding of Divine power and goodness. It does the the theologian no good  to argue for the falsity of Christianity on the existence of an omnipotent, omniscient, pleasure-maximizer is incompossible with a world such as ours, because Christians never believed God was a pleasure-maximizer anyway. But equally, the truth of Christianity would be inadequately defended by the observation that an omnipotent, omniscient egoist could have created a world with suffering creatures because Christians insist that God loves other(created) than Himself. The extension of'evil' in (2) in likewise important. Since Mackie and his successors are out to show that 'the several parts of the essential theological doctrine are inconsistent each other',4 they can accomplish their aim only if circumscribe the extension of 'evil' as their religious opponents do. By the same token, it is not enough for Chris philosophers to explain how the power, knowledge, goodness of God could coexist with some evils or other; a full account must exhibit the compossibility of Divine perfection with evils in the amounts and of the kinds found in the actual world (and evaluated as such by Christian standards).
The moral of my earlier story might be summarized where the internal coherence of a system of religious belief stake, successful arguments for its inconsistency must draw on premisses (explicitly or implicitly) internal to that system obviously acceptable to its adherents; likewise for succ rebuttals or explanations of consistency. The thrust argument is to push both sides of the debate towards detailed attention to and subtle understanding of the religious system in question.
As a Christian philosopher, I want to focus in this paper
Read above text from Marilyn McCord Adams, ‘Horrendous Evils and the Goodness of God’ . Answer question and include quotes: 1) On pp. 297-98 Marilyn McCord Adams outlines her previous response to Mackie’s article ‘Evil and Omnipotence’. Summarize her first response to Mackie’s paper. (3-4 sentences)

In her previous response to Mackie's article "Evil and Omnipotence," Marilyn McCord Adams emphasizes the necessity for both defenders and critics of religious belief to ensure that their arguments accurately reflect the specific religious tradition's understanding of divine attributes, such as power and goodness. She argues that it is not sufficient to simply assert that the existence of an omnipotent, omniscient God is incompatible with the presence of evil without considering the precise nature of divine perfection and the specific kinds of evil recognized by the religious tradition in question. Adams points out that a successful challenge to the coherence of a religious belief system must draw on premises that are both internally consistent with that system and acceptable to its adherents, emphasizing the importance of a detailed and nuanced understanding of the religious framework being discussed. She writes, "care must be taken, both by the atheologians who deploy this argument and the believers who defend against it, to insure that the operative attribute-analyses accurately reflect that religion’s understanding of Divine power and goodness," and "a full account must exhibit the compossibility of Divine perfection with evils in the amounts and of the kinds found in the actual world (and evaluated as such by Christian standards)."

problem for the truth of Christianity raised by what I shall call 'horrendous' evils. Although our world is riddled with them, the Biblical record punctuated by them, and one of them-viz., the passion of Christ, according to Christian belief, the judicial murder of God by the people of God-is memorialized by the Church on its most solemn holiday (Good Friday) and in its central sacrament (the Eucharist), the problem of horrendous evils is largely skirted by standard treatments for the good reason that they are intractable by them. After showing why, I will draw on other Christian materials to sketch ways of meeting this, the deepest of religious problems.
II
Defining the Category. For present purposes, I define 'horrendous evils' as 'evils the participation in (the doing or suffering of) which gives one reason prima facie to doubt whether one's life could (given their inclusion in it) be a great good to one on the whole'. Such reasonable doubt arises because it is so difficult humanly to conceive how such evils could be overcome. Borrowing Chisholm's contrast between balancing off (w occurs when the opposing values of mutually exclusive parts whole partially or totally cancel each other out) and de (which cannot occur by the mere addition to the whole of a part of opposing value, but involves some 'organic unity' am the values of parts and wholes, as when the positive aestheric value of a whole painting defeats the ugliness of a small colour patch)5, horrendous evils seem prima facie, not only to balance off but to engulf the positive value of a participant's l Nevertheless, that very horrendous proportion, by which they threaten to rob a person's life of positive meaning, cries out only to be engulfed, but to be made meaningful through positive and decisive defeat.
I understand this criterion to be objective, but relative to individuals. The example of habitual complainers, who how to make the worst of a good situation, shows individuals not to be incorrigible experts on what ills would defeat the value of their lives. Nevertheless, nature and experience people with different strengths; one bears easily what crushes another. 
And a major consideration in determining whetehr individual's life is/has been a great good whole, is invariably and appropriately how it has seemed to him/her.6I offer the following list of paradigmatic horrors: the rape of a woman and axing off of her arms, psychophysical torture whose ultimate goal is the disintegration of personality, betrayal of one's deepest loyalties, cannibalizing one's own off-spring, child abuse of the sort described by Ivan Karamazov, child pornography,  parental incest, slow death by starvation, participation in the Nazi death camps, the explosion of nuclear bombs over populated areas, having to choose which of one’s children shall live and which be executed by terrorists and/or unwitting agent of the disfigurement or death of those one loves best. I regard these as paradigmatic because I believe most people would find in the doing or suffering of them prima facie reason to doubt the positive meaning of their livers.  Christian belief counts the crucifixion of Christ another. On the one hand, death by crucifixion seemed to defeat Jesus’ Messianic vocation; for according to Jewish law, death by  hanging from a tree made its victim ritually accursed, definitively excluded from the compass of  God’s people, a fortiori disqualified from being the Messiah. On the other hand, it represented the defeat of its perpetrators' as those who were to prepare the people of coming, kill and ritually accurse the true MEssiah, according to later theological understanding, God Himself
III
The Impotence of Standard Solutions. For better or worse, now-standard strategies for 'solving' the problem of evil are powerless in the face of horrendous evils.
.1 Seeking the Reason Why In his model article 'Hume on Evil',' Pike takes up Mackie's challenge, arguing that (p1) fails 
Read above text from Marilyn McCord Adams, ‘Horrendous Evils and the Goodness of God’ . Answer question and include quotes: 2) How does Adams define ‘horrendous evils’ (pp. 299-300)? What do you think she means when she says ‘horrendous evils seem prima facie, not only to balance off but to engulf the positive value of a participant’s life’ (p. 299)? (3-4 sentences)

Marilyn McCord Adams defines 'horrendous evils' as "evils the participation in (the doing or suffering of) which gives one reason prima facie to doubt whether one's life could (given their inclusion in it) be a great good to one on the whole." This definition highlights these evils' profound impact, suggesting their capacity to fundamentally challenge the meaningfulness or goodness of a person’s life due to their sheer atrociousness and the depth of suffering they entail. When Adams says that "horrendous evils seem prima facie, not only to balance off but to engulf the positive value of a participant’s life," she implies that these evils are so deeply traumatic and overwhelming that they appear to negate or consume any positive aspects of life, threatening to leave the individual unable to perceive their life as having overall positive value. This engulfing nature goes beyond merely balancing out positive and negative experiences, suggesting a profound existential threat to seeing one's life as good or meaningful in any significant way.

The Insufficiency of Global Defeat. A pair order here: (i) between two dimensions of Divine goodness in relation to creation-viz., 'producer of 'global goods’ and ‘goodness to' or 'love of individual created persons’; and (ii) between the overbalance/defeat of evil by good on the global scale, and the overbalance/defeat of evil by good within the context of an individual person's life.'2 Correspondingly, we may separate two problems of evil parallel to goodness mentioned in (i).
In effect, generic and global approaches are directed to the first problem: they defend Divine goodness along the first (global) dimension by suggesting logically possible strategies for the global defeat of evils. But establishing God’s excellence as a producer of global goods does not auto second problem, especially in a world containing horrendous evils. For God cannot be said to be good or loving to any created persons the positive meaning of whose lives He allows to be engulfed in and/or defeated by evils--that is, individuals within whose lives horrendous evils remain undefeated. Yet, the only way unsupplemented global and generic approaches could have to explain the latter, would be by applying their general reasons-why to particular cases of horrendous suffering.
Unfortunately, such an exercise fails to give satisfaction. Suppose for the sake of argument that horrendous evil could be included in maximally perfect world orders; its being partially constitutive of such an order would assign it that generic and global positive meaning. But would knowledge of such a fact,  defeat for a mother the prima facie reason provided by her cannibalism of her own infant, to wish that she never been born? Again, the aim of perfect retributive balance confers meaning on evils imposed. But would knowledge that the torturer was being tortured give the victim who broke down and turned traitor under pressure, any more reason to think his/her life worthwhile? Would it not merely multiply reasons for the torturer to doubt that his/her life could turn out to be a good to him/her on the whole? Could the truck-driver who accidentally runs over his beloved child find consolation in the idea that this middle-known'" but unintended side-effect was part of price God accepted for a world with the best balance of moral good over moral evil He could get?
Not only does the application to horrors of such generic a global reasons for Divine permission of evils fail to solve second problem of evil; it makes it worse by adding generic prima facie reasons to doubt whether human life would be a great go to individual human beings in possible worlds where such Divine motives were operative. For, taken in isolation and make to bear the weight of the whole explanation, such reasons-w draw a picture of Divine indifference or even hostility to the human plight. Would the fact that God permitted horrors because they were constitutive means to His end of glob perfection, or that He tolerated them because He could obtain that global end anyway, make the participant's life more tolerable, more worth living for him/her? Given radical human vulnerability to horrendous evils, the ease with which human participate in them, whether as victim or perpetrator, would the thought that God visits horrors on anyone who caused them, simply because s/he deserves it, provide one more reason to expect human life to be a nightmare?
Those willing to split the two problems of evil apart might adopt a divide-and-conquer strategy, by simply denying Divine goodness along the second dimension. For example, many Christians do not believe that God will insure an overwhelming good life to each and every person He creates. Some say the decisive defeat of evil with good is promised only within the lives of the obedient, who enter by the narrow gate. Some speculate that the elect may be few. Many recognize that the sufferings of this present life are as nothing compared to the hell of eternal torment, designed to defeat goodness with horrors within the lives of the damned. Such a road can be consistently travelled only at the heavy  toll of admitting that human life in world such as ours is a bad bet. Imagine (adapting Rawls' device) pers position in a pre-original position, considering possible worlds containing managers of differing power, wisdom, and character, an character, and subjects of varying fates. The question they are to answer about each world is whether they would willingly enter it as a human being, from behind a veil of ignorance as to which position they would occupy. Reason would, I submit, dictate a negative verdict for worlds whose omniscient and omnipotent manager permits pre-mortem horrors that remain undefeated within the context of the human participant's life; a fortiori, for worlds in which so or most humans suffer eternal torment.
Inaccessible Reasons. So far, I have argued that generic and global solutions are at best incomplete: however well their account of Divine motivating reasons deals with the first problem of evil, the attempt to extend it to the second fails, making it worse. This verdict might seem prima facie tolerable to standard generic and global approaches and indicative of only minor modification in their strategy: let the above-mentioned generic and global reasons cover Divine permission of horrendous evils, and find other reasons compatible with per goodness why even an omnipotent, omniscient God wo permit horrors.
In my judgment, such an approach is hopeless. As Plantinga points out, where horrendous evils are concerned, not only do we not know God's actual reason for permitting them; we cannot even conceive of any plausible candidate sort of reason consistant with worthwhile lives for human participants in them.
IV
The How of God's Victory. Up to now my discussion has given the reader cause to wonder whose side I am on anyway? For I hvea insisted, with rebels like Ivan Karamazov andJohn Stuart Mill, on spot-lighting the problem horrendous evils pose. Yet, I have signalled my preference for a vision of Christianity that insists on both dimensions of Divine goodness, and maintains not only (a)

Read above text from Marilyn McCord Adams, ‘Horrendous Evils and the Goodness of God’ . Answer question and include quotes: 3) At the top of p. 302 Adams makes this pair of distinctions:

(i) God as ‘producer of global goods’ / God as ‘goodness to’ individual persons 
and 
(ii) ‘overbalance/defeat’ of evil on a global scale / ‘overbalance/defeat’ of evil on an individual scale

and she thinks that many solutions to the problem of evil focus on the first element of these two pairs (i.e., the global).

What does she think are some of the problems of this focus on the first element of the pairs (God as producer of global goods; overbalance or defeat of evil on a global scale)? (pp. 303-304) (2-3 sentences)

Marilyn McCord Adams identifies several problems with focusing on the first element of the pairs, specifically on God as a producer of global goods and the overbalance or defeat of evil on a global scale. She argues that this focus fails to address the "second problem" of evil, which involves the individual experience of horrendous evils that engulf the positive meaning of a person’s life. Adams points out that even if horrendous evils could be justified on a global scale as part of a maximally perfect world order, this does not alleviate the personal suffering or provide meaningful consolation to individuals who experience such evils. As she poignantly questions, "But would knowledge of such a fact, for example, defeat for a mother the prima facie reason provided by her cannibalism of her own infant, to wish that she never had been born?" This highlights the insufficiency of global justifications in addressing the deeply personal and devastating impact of horrendous evils on individuals.

that God will be good enough to created persons to make human life a good bet, but also (b) that each created person will have a life that is a great good to him/her on the whole. My critique of standard approaches to the problem of evil thus seems to reinforce atheologian Mackie's verdict of 'positive irrationality' for such a religious position.
4.1 Whys versus Hows. The inaccessibility of reasons-why seems especially decisive. For surely an all-wise and all-powerful God, who loved each created person enough (a) to defeat any experienced horrors within the context of the participant's life, and (b) to give each created person a life that is a great good to him/her on the whole, would not permit such persons to suffer horrors for no reason.'" Does not our inability even to conceive of plausible candidate reasons suffice to make belief in such a God positively irrational in a world containing horrors? In my judgment, it does not.
To be sure, motivating reasons come in several varieties relative to our conceptual grasp: There are (i) reasons of the sort we can readily understand when we are informed of them (e.g., the mother who permits her child to undergo painful heart surgery because it is the only humanly possible way to save its life). Moreover, there are (ii) reasons we would be cognitively, emotionally, and spiritually equipped to grasp if only we had a larger memory or wider attention span (analogy: I may be able to memorize small town street plans; memorizing the road networks of the entire country is a task requiring more of the same, in the way that proving Godel's theorem is not). Some generic and global approaches insinuate that Divine permission of evils has motivating reasons of this sort. Finally, (iii) there are reasons that we are cognitively, emotionally, and/or spiritually too immature to fathom (the way a two-year old child is incapable of understanding its mother's reasons for permitting the surgery). I agree with Plantinga that our ignorance of Divine reasons for permitting horrendous evils is not of types (i) or (ii), but of type (iii).
Nevertheless, if there are varieties of ignorance, there are also varieties of reassurance. The two-year old heart patient is convinced of its mother's love, not by her cognitively inaccessible reasons, but by her intimate care and presence through its painful experience. The story ofJob suggests something similar is true with human participation in horrendous suffering. God does not give Job His reasons-why, and implies that JOb isn’t smart enough to grasp them; rather Job is lectured on the extent of Divine power, and sees God's goodness face to face! likewise,  I suggest, to exhibit the logical compossibility of both dimensions  of Divine goodness with horrendous suffering, it is not necessary to find logically possible reasons why God is enough to show how God can be good persons despite their participation in horrors- by defeating them within the context of the individual’s life and by giving that individual a life that is a great the whole.
What sort of Valuables? In my opinion, the reasonableness Christianity can be maintained in the face of horrendous ev only by drawing on resources of religious value theory. For way for God to be good to created persons is by relating th appropriately to relevant and great goods. But philosophical and religious theories differ importantly on what valuables they admit into their ontology. Some maintain that 'what you see is what you get', but nevertheless admit a wide range of valuables, from sensory pleasures, the beauty of nature and culture artifacts, the joys of creativity, to loving personal intimacy. Others posit a transcendent good (e.g. the Form of the Good Platonism, or God, the Supremely Valuable Object, in Christianity). In the spirit of Ivan Karamazov, I am convinced that the depth of horrific evil cannot be accurately estimated without recognizing it to be incommensurate with any pack of merely non-transcendent goods and so unable to be balanced off, much less defeated thereby.
Where the internal coherence of Christianity is the issue, however, it is fair to appeal to its own store of valuables. Fr Christian point of view, God is a being a greater than which cannot be conceived, a good incommensurate with both created goods and temporal evils. Likewise, the good of beatific, face face intimacy with God is simply incommensurate with merely non-transcendent goods or ills a person might experience .  Read above text from Marilyn McCord Adams, ‘Horrendous Evils and the Goodness of God’ . Answer question and include quotes: 4) In section 4.1 (pp. 305-306) of this article, Adams discusses issues surrounding our cognitive powers and their limitations and the possibility of there being reassurance in the midst of suffering without having ‘reasons-why’ or explanations for the necessity of suffering. What image does she offer for how one might understand the compatibility between lack of ‘reasons-why’ suffering occurs and some type of reassurance? How does she think this image aligns with the story of the Book of Job? (2-3 sentences)

Marilyn McCord Adams offers the image of a two-year-old undergoing necessary but painful heart surgery as an analogy for understanding the compatibility between the lack of 'reasons-why' for suffering and the provision of reassurance. In this analogy, the child cannot grasp the cognitive reasons for the surgery, but feels reassured by the mother's loving presence and intimate care. Adams correlates this image to the story of Job from the Bible, highlighting that just as God does not provide Job with explicit reasons for his sufferings but demonstrates His greatness and presence, individuals experiencing horrendous evils may not understand the divine rationale but can find reassurance in the intimate and caring presence of God. Adams states, "The two-year-old heart patient is convinced of its mother's love, not by her cognitively inaccessible reasons, but by her intimate care and presence through its painful experience... Job is lectured on the extent of Divine power, and sees God's goodness face to face!" This suggests a paradigm where reassurance is not found in understanding the 'whys' but in experiencing a relationship that transcends cognitive explanation.

Thus, the good of beatific face-to-face intimacy with God wo engulf(in a sense analogous to Chisholmian balancing off) even the horrendous evils humans experience in this present life here below, and overcome any prima facie reasons the individual had to doubt whether his/her life would or could be worth living.
Personal Meaning, Horrors Defeated. Engulfing personal horrors within the context of the participant's life would vouchsafe to that individual a life that was a great good to him/her on the whole. I am still inclined to think it would guarantee that immeasurable Divine goodness to any person thus benefited. But there is good theological reason for Christians to believe that God would go further, beyond engulfment to defeat. For it is the nature of persons to look for meaning, both in their lives and in the world. Divine respect for and commitment to created personhood would drive God to make all those sufferings which threaten to destroy the positive meaning of a person's life meaningful through positive defeat.
How could God do it? So far as I can see, only by integrating participation in horrendous evils into a person's relationship with God. Possible dimensions of integration are charted by Christian soteriology. I pause here to sketch three'6. (i) First, because God in Christ participated in horrendous evil through His passion and death, human experience of horrors can be a means of identifying with Christ, either through sympathetic identification (in which each person suffers his/her own pains, but their similarity enables each to know what it is like for the other) or through mystical identification (in which the created person is supposed literally to experience a share of Christ's                  Read above text from Marilyn McCord Adams, ‘Horrendous Evils and the Goodness of God’ . Answer question and include quotes: 5) What does Adams think might ‘engulf’ horrendous evils, and how would it do so? (p. 307) (2-3 sentences)

Marilyn McCord Adams suggests that the good of beatific face-to-face intimacy with God would "engulf" the horrendous evils humans experience, analogous to Chisholmian balancing off, thereby overcoming any prima facie reasons individuals might have to doubt whether their lives were or could be worth living. She explains this engulfment as a process by which divine goodness, through direct, intimate relationship with God, would not just balance but decisively overcome the weight of horrendous evils in a person’s life, making those lives overwhelmingly positive on the whole. Adams states, "Thus, the good of beatific face-to-face intimacy with God would engulf... even the horrendous evils humans experience in this present life here below, and overcome any prima facie reasons the individual had to doubt whether his/her life would or could be worth living."

Thus, the good of beatific face-to-face intimacy with God wo engulf(in a sense analogous to Chisholmian balancing off) even the horrendous evils humans experience in this present life here below, and overcome any prima facie reasons the individual had to doubt whether his/her life would or could be worth living.
Personal Meaning, Horrors Defeated. Engulfing personal horrors within the context of the participant's life would vouchsafe to that individual a life that was a great good to him/her on the whole. I am still inclined to think it would guarantee that immeasurable Divine goodness to any person thus benefited. But there is good theological reason for Christians to believe that God would go further, beyond engulfment to defeat. For it is the nature of persons to look for meaning, both in their lives and in the world. Divine respect for and commitment to created personhood would drive God to make all those sufferings which threaten to destroy the positive meaning of a person's life meaningful through positive defeat.
How could God do it? So far as I can see, only by integrating participation in horrendous evils into a person's relationship with God. Possible dimensions of integration are charted by Christian soteriology. I pause here to sketch three'6. (i) First, because God in Christ participated in horrendous evil through His passion and death, human experience of horrors can be a means of identifying with Christ, either through sympathetic identification (in which each person suffers his/her own pains, but their similarity enables each to know what it is like for the other) or through mystical identification (in which the created person is supposed literally to experience a share of Christ's pain"). (ii) Julian of Norwich's description of heavenly welcom suggests the possible defeat of horrendous evil through Divine gratitude. According to Julian, before the elect have a chance to thank God for all He has done for them, God will say, “Thank you for all your suffering, the suffering of your youth’. She says that the creature's experience of Divine gratitude will bring such full and unending joy as could not be merited by the whole sea of human pain and suffering throughout the ages. (iii) A third idea identifies temporal suffering itself with a vision into  the inner life of God, and can be developed several ways.  Perhaps, contrary to medieval theology, God is not impassible  but rather has matched capacities for joy and for suffering. Perhaps, as the Heidelberg catechism suggests, GOd responds to  human sin and the sufferings of Christ with an agony beyond  human conception."9 Alternatively, the inner life of God may be, strictly speaking and in and of itself, beyond both joy and sorrow. But, just as (according to Rudolf Otto) humans experience Divine presence now as tremendum (with deep dread  and anxiety), now as fascinans (with ineffable attraction), so perhaps our deepest suffering as much as our highest joeys may themselves be direct visions into the inner life of God, imperfect but somehow less obscure in proportion to their intensity. And if  a face-to-face vision of God is a good for humans incommensurate  with any non-transcendent goods or ills, so any vision of God (including horrendous suffering) would have a good aspect insofar as it is a vision of God (even if it has an evil aspect insofar as it is horrendous suffering). For the most recognized as experiences of God (any more than the city slicker recognizes his visual image of a brown patch as a vision of Beulah the cow in the distance). But, Christian mysticism might claim, at least from the post-mortem perspective of the beatific vision, such sufferings will be seen for what they were and retrospectively no one will wish away any intimate encounters 


with God from his/her life-history of this world. The created person's experience of the beatific vision together with his/ knowledge that intimate Divine presence stretched back over his/her pre-mortem life and reached down into the depths of his/her worst suffering, would provide comfort retrospective comfort independent of comprehension of the reasons-why akin to the two-year-old's assurance of its mother's love. Taking this third approach, Christians would not need to commit themselves about what in any event we do not know: viz., whether we will (like the two-year-old) ever grow up enough to understand the reasons why God permits our participation in horrendous evils. For by contrast with the best of earthly mothers, such Divi intimacy is an incommensurate good and would cancel out for the creature any need to know why.
V
Conclusion. The worst evils demand to be defeated by the be goods. Horrendous evils can be overcome only by the goodness of God. Relative to human nature, participation in horrendous evils and loving intimacy with God are alike disproportionate: for the former threatens to engulf the good in an individual human life with evil, while the latter guarantees the reverse engulfment of evil by good. Relative to one another, there is also disproportion, because the good that God is, and intimate relationship with Him, is incommensurate with created goods and evils alike. Because intimacy with God so outscales relations (good or bad) with any creatures, integration into the human person's relationship with God confers significant meaning positive value even on horrendous suffering. This result coheres with basic Christian intuition: that the powers of darkness are stronger than humans, but they are no match for God!
Standard generic and global solutions have for the most part tried to operate within the territory common to believer and believer, within the confines of religion-neutral value theory. Many discussions reflect the hope that substitute attribute-analyses, candidate reasons-why and/or defeaters could issue out of values shared by believers and unbelievers alike. And some virtually make this a requirement on an adequate solution. Mackie knew better how to distinguish the many charges that may be levelled against religion. Just as philosophers may or may not find the existence 

Read above text from Marilyn McCord Adams, ‘Horrendous Evils and the Goodness of God’ . Answer question and include quotes: 6) What are the three possible scenarios Adams gives for illustrating how God might ‘defeat’ horrendous evils without necessarily providing ‘reasons-why’ or explanations for suffering? (pp. 307-309) (3-4 sentences)

Marilyn McCord Adams outlines three scenarios for how God could 'defeat' horrendous evils, providing depth and personal meaning to suffering without necessarily offering clear 'reasons-why'. The first scenario involves identification with Christ, where human experiences of horror facilitate a connection either through sympathetic identification, feeling a shared experience of pain, or through mystical identification, literally sharing in Christ’s suffering. The second scenario draws on Julian of Norwich's concept of Divine gratitude, where God thanks individuals for their sufferings, and this Divine acknowledgment brings unending joy that far outweighs the sea of human pain. The third scenario suggests that temporal sufferings might offer a vision into the inner life of God, possibly reflecting God's own capacities for joy and suffering or offering a direct though imperfect glimpse into the divine essence, which gains a good aspect insofar as it is a vision of God, despite also being an experience of horrendous suffering. Through these scenarios, Adams proposes that a profound, meaningful relationship with God can transform and 'defeat' the negative impact of horrendous evils in a person’s life.
include quotes into answers

Marilyn McCord Adams suggests three detailed scenarios for how God might 'defeat' horrendous evils, transforming profound suffering without necessarily offering 'reasons-why' or superficial explanations:

1. **Identification with Christ**: Adams proposes that through experiencing horrors, humans can identify with Christ either through "sympathetic identification (in which each person suffers his/her own pains, but their similarity enables each to know what it is like for the other)" or "mystical identification (in which the created person is supposed literally to experience a share of Christ's pain)."

2. **Divine Gratitude as Described by Julian of Norwich**: In this scenario, the transcendent experience of Divine gratitude directly addresses human suffering, where "God will say, 'Thank you for all your suffering, the suffering of your youth'." Adams references Julian’s vision, where the acknowledgment and resulting joy from God for one's suffering in life bring a fullness of joy “as could not be merited by the whole sea of human pain and suffering throughout the ages.”

3. **Temporal Suffering Offering a Vision into the Inner Life of God**: Here, Adams explores the notion that suffering could be a form of intimate knowledge of God, saying, "Perhaps, as the Heidelberg catechism suggests, God responds to human sin and the sufferings of Christ with an agony beyond human conception." This scenario suggests that in our deepest suffering, as well as in our highest joys, we might be experiencing "direct visions into the inner life of God, imperfect but somehow less obscure in proportion to their intensity."

Through these scenarios, Adams argues for a deeply relational and transformative understanding of suffering that allows individuals to find profound personal meaning, even in the midst of horrendous evils, without the necessity of comprehensible 'reasons-why' from God.
in one paragraph , 3-4 sentences, include quotes

Marilyn McCord Adams, in her exploration of God's potential to 'defeat' horrendous evils without offering explicit 'reasons-why,' presents three innovative scenarios. First, she posits that "human experience of horrors can be a means of identifying with Christ," allowing for either "sympathetic" or "mystical identification" with Christ's suffering, thereby integrating personal sufferings with a significant religious narrative. She then turns to Julian of Norwich's vision of Divine gratitude, where God's first words to the elect are "Thank you for all your suffering," transforming suffering through divine acknowledgment. Lastly, she suggests that suffering could be an intimate glimpse into "the inner life of God," where even "our deepest suffering as much as our highest joys may themselves be direct visions into the inner life of God." Through these scenarios, Adams frames horrendous evils not merely as obstacles to faith but as profound opportunities for deepening the relationship between humans and the divine, emphasizing the transformative potential of suffering within a religious context.

Adams criticizes that "Divine permission of evils on a global scale automatically translates to the assurance of a great good life for individual human beings," suggesting it could imply "Divine indifference or even hostility towards human suffering."  is this appeared in text provided?  is it right?

The statement you've provided captures the essence of Marilyn McCord Adams' argument but isn't a direct quote from the text excerpts previously provided. Adams indeed criticizes the insufficiency of global and generic explanations for the problem of evil, particularly when it comes to addressing individual suffering caused by horrendous evils. She argues that such explanations fail to assure that individual lives engulfed by such evils can still be deemed good on the whole. Specifically, she points out that applying global reasons for the existence of evil to individual cases of horrendous suffering does not satisfactorily address the personal impact of those evils, and might even exacerbate the problem by suggesting a form of divine indifference or hostility towards human plight. Adams emphasizes the need for responses to the problem of evil that consider the intimate and personal dimensions of human suffering and God’s relationship with individuals, rather than relying solely on abstract, universal justifications. However, the exact phrases "Divine permission of evils on a global scale automatically translates to the assurance of a great good life for individual human beings," and suggesting it could imply "Divine indifference or even hostility towards human suffering," are interpretative summaries rather than direct quotations from the provided excerpts.

On problems with focusing on the first element of the pairs  (God as producer of global goods; overbalance or defeat of evil on a global scale), Adam argues that this focus fails to address the “second problem” of evil, which involves the individual experience of horrendous evils that engulf the positive meaning of a person’s life. Adams points out that even suppose horrendous evils could be included in the context of a global scale as part of a maximally perfect world order, this does not reduce the personal suffering or provide meaningful comfort to individuals who experience such evils, or might even worsen the problem by suggesting a form of “divine indifference or hostility towards human plight”. highlighting the insufficiency of global justifications to address the deeply personal and traumatic impact of horrendous evils on individuals – “But would knowledge of such a fact, for example, defeat for a mother the prima facie reason provided by her cannibalism of her own infant, to wish that she never had been born?”

and she thinks that many solutions to the problem of evil focus on the first element of these two pairs (i.e., the global).

Yes, your summary accurately reflects Marilyn McCord Adams' arguments regarding the problems with focusing on the first element of the pairs (God as producer of global goods and the overbalance or defeat of evil on a global scale). Adams criticizes this focus for its failure to address the "second problem" of evil, which relates to the individual's experience of horrendous evils that significantly impact their personal lives. She highlights that such a global perspective might not alleviate the personal suffering or offer meaningful comfort to those who endure these evils, and indeed, could exacerbate the issue by implying a form of "divine indifference or hostility towards human plight." Your inclusion of the question regarding whether knowledge of evil's role in a global context could provide comfort to a mother who has experienced profound loss underscores Adams' point about the inadequacy of global justifications in addressing deeply personal and traumatic experiences of evil.

The Insufficiency of Global Defeat. A pair order here: (i) between two dimensions of Divine goodness in relation to creation-viz., 'producer of ‘global goods’ and ‘goodness to’ or ‘love of individual created persons’; and (ii) between the overbalance/defeat of evil by good on the global scale, and the overbalance/defeat of evil by good within the context of an individual person’s life.‘2 Correspondingly, we may separate two problems of evil parallel to goodness mentioned in (i).
In effect, generic and global approaches are directed to the first problem: they defend Divine goodness along the first (global) dimension by suggesting logically possible strategies for the global defeat of evils. But establishing God’s excellence as a producer of global goods does not auto second problem, especially in a world containing horrendous evils. For God cannot be said to be good or loving to any created persons the positive meaning of whose lives He allows to be engulfed in and/or defeated by evils–that is, individuals within whose lives horrendous evils remain undefeated. Yet, the only way unsupplemented global and generic approaches could have to explain the latter, would be by applying their general reasons-why to particular cases of horrendous suffering.
Unfortunately, such an exercise fails to give satisfaction. Suppose for the sake of argument that horrendous evil could be included in maximally perfect world orders; its being partially constitutive of such an order would assign it that generic and global positive meaning. But would knowledge of such a fact, defeat for a mother the prima facie reason provided by her cannibalism of her own infant, to wish that she never been born? Again, the aim of perfect retributive balance confers meaning on evils imposed. But would knowledge that the torturer was being tortured give the victim who broke down and turned traitor under pressure, any more reason to think his/her life worthwhile? Would it not merely multiply reasons for the torturer to doubt that his/her life could turn out to be a good to him/her on the whole? Could the truck-driver who accidentally runs over his beloved child find consolation in the idea that this middle-known’" but unintended side-effect was part of price God accepted for a world with the best balance of moral good over moral evil He could get?
Not only does the application to horrors of such generic a global reasons for Divine permission of evils fail to solve second problem of evil; it makes it worse by adding generic prima facie reasons to doubt whether human life would be a great go to individual human beings in possible worlds where such Divine motives were operative. For, taken in isolation and make to bear the weight of the whole explanation, such reasons-w draw a picture of Divine indifference or even hostility to the human plight. Would the fact that God permitted horrors because they were constitutive means to His end of glob perfection, or that He tolerated them because He could obtain that global end anyway, make the participant’s life more tolerable, more worth living for him/her? Given radical human vulnerability to horrendous evils, the ease with which human participate in them, whether as victim or perpetrator, would the thought that God visits horrors on anyone who caused them, simply because s/he deserves it, provide one more reason to expect human life to be a nightmare?
Those willing to split the two problems of evil apart might adopt a divide-and-conquer strategy, by simply denying Divine goodness along the second dimension. For example, many Christians do not believe that God will insure an overwhelming good life to each and every person He creates. Some say the decisive defeat of evil with good is promised only within the lives of the obedient, who enter by the narrow gate. Some speculate that the elect may be few. Many recognize that the sufferings of this present life are as nothing compared to the hell of eternal torment, designed to defeat goodness with horrors within the lives of the damned. Such a road can be consistently travelled only at the heavy toll of admitting that human life in world such as ours is a bad bet. Imagine (adapting Rawls’ device) pers position in a pre-original position, considering possible worlds containing managers of differing power, wisdom, and character, an character, and subjects of varying fates. The question they are to answer about each world is whether they would willingly enter it as a human being, from behind a veil of ignorance as to which position they would occupy. Reason would, I submit, dictate a negative verdict for worlds whose omniscient and omnipotent manager permits pre-mortem horrors that remain undefeated within the context of the human participant’s life; a fortiori, for worlds in which so or most humans suffer eternal torment.
Inaccessible Reasons. So far, I have argued that generic and global solutions are at best incomplete: however well their account of Divine motivating reasons deals with the first problem of evil, the attempt to extend it to the second fails, making it worse. This verdict might seem prima facie tolerable to standard generic and global approaches and indicative of only minor modification in their strategy: let the above-mentioned generic and global reasons cover Divine permission of horrendous evils, and find other reasons compatible with per goodness why even an omnipotent, omniscient God wo permit horrors.
In my judgment, such an approach is hopeless. As Plantinga points out, where horrendous evils are concerned, not only do we not know God’s actual reason for permitting them; we cannot even conceive of any plausible candidate sort of reason consistant with worthwhile lives for human participants in them.
IV
The How of God’s Victory. Up to now my discussion has given the reader cause to wonder whose side I am on anyway? For I hvea insisted, with rebels like Ivan Karamazov andJohn Stuart Mill, on spot-lighting the problem horrendous evils pose. Yet, I have signalled my preference for a vision of Christianity that insists on both dimensions of Divine goodness, and maintains not only (a)

and she thinks that many solutions to the problem of evil focus on the first element of these two pairs (i.e., the global).

What does she think are some of the problems of this focus on the first element of the pairs (God as producer of global goods; overbalance or defeat of evil on a global scale)? (pp. 303-304) (2-3 sentences)

Marilyn McCord Adams critiques the focus on the first elements of the pairs, emphasizing that it overlooks the individual's dire experience of horrendous evils, which can profoundly negate the perceived value and goodness of one's life. She points out that "generic and global approaches" fail to address the intimate, devastating impact of such evils on individual lives, suggesting that these approaches might inadvertently convey a "picture of Divine indifference or even hostility to the human plight." Adams articulates the insufficiency of this global perspective with poignant illustrations, questioning, for example, whether a mother who has suffered through the horrendous evil of cannibalizing her own infant could find her suffering justified or ameliorated by global goods, thereby highlighting the critical gap in addressing the deeply personal anguish and moral questioning spawned by such evils.

Marilyn McCord Adams critiques the focus on the global scale of Divine goodness and the overbalance of evil by good globally, highlighting its inadequacy in providing solace or justification for individual suffering due to horrendous evils. She explicitly states, "For God cannot be said to be good or loving to any created persons the positive meaning of whose lives He allows to be engulfed in and/or defeated by evils—that is, individuals within whose lives horrendous evils remain undefeated." Adams further emphasizes the disconnect between global justification and personal suffering by challenging the notion that knowledge of evil's part in a global plan could comfort or justify individual horrors, asking, "But would knowledge of such a fact, for example, defeat for a mother the prima facie reason provided by her cannibalism of her own infant, to wish that she never had been born?" This question underscores her argument that global approaches fail to address the "second problem" of evil, adding to a "picture of Divine indifference or even hostility to the human plight."

(i) God as ‘producer of global goods’ / God as ‘goodness to’ individual persons

and she thinks that many solutions to the problem of evil focus on the first element of these two pairs (i.e., the global).

What does she think are some of the problems of this focus on the first element of the pairs (God as producer of global goods; overbalance or defeat of evil on a global scale)? (pp. 303-304) (2-3 sentences)

Marilyn McCord Adams criticizes the focus on the first element of the pairs, highlighting that while solutions may defend Divine goodness on a global scale, they fail to adequately address the individual suffering caused by horrendous evils. She articulates this concern by stating, "For God cannot be said to be good or loving to any created persons the positive meaning of whose lives He allows to be engulfed in and/or defeated by evils—that is, individuals within whose lives horrendous evils remain undefeated." Adams further emphasizes the disconnect between global solutions and individual experiences of suffering by questioning the consolation such global perspectives might offer to individuals who have endured profound personal horrors, illustrating this with poignant examples such as, "But would knowledge of such a fact, for example, defeat for a mother the prima facie reason provided by her cannibalism of her own infant, to wish that she never had been born?" These reflections underline her criticism of global approaches as insufficient for addressing the deeply personal and destructive impact of horrendous evils on individuals, suggesting they may even exacerbate the issue by implying a form of divine indifference or hostility towards human suffering.

Marilyn McCord Adams critiques the emphasis on globally focused solutions to the problem of evil, arguing that they inadequately address the profound individual suffering caused by horrendous evils. She points out, "For God cannot be said to be good or loving to any created persons the positive meaning of whose lives He allows to be engulfed in and/or defeated by evils—that is, individuals whose lives horrendous evils remain undefeated." Adams highlights the ineffectiveness of global justifications in providing comfort to those who have endured personal horrors, exemplified in her question, "But would knowledge of such a fact, for example, defeat for a mother the prima facie reason provided by her cannibalism of her own infant, to wish that she never had been born?" Moreover, she argues that relying solely on global justifications not only fails to resolve but exacerbates the problem by "adding generic prima facie reasons to doubt whether human life would be a great good to individual human beings in possible worlds where such Divine motives were operative." This assertion underscores her critique that such approaches, by neglecting the individual dimension of suffering, might unintentionally suggest divine indifference or even hostility toward human plight.
